Okay, so first thing. When you think about your toolbox, you really only have three drawers in it, right? You've got the product itself and the message about it, which I consider to be seamless. If you want to think about it as four drawers, I guess you can, but I consider it to be seamless. So you have your opportunity, or if you must, your improvement, or if you must, your repair, the thing itself. Okay? And you've got to give some thought now to, is the thing itself right? Is the thing itself the best thing it can be for you to present it with opportunity language and for it to have the most appeal to the most number of people in your market that you want to sell it to, to your target audience? So you've got to give some thought to the thing itself. You may want to make modifications to your thing. Your second drawer is process, okay? So that is now how we make the sale, right? How we sell things. A trend, for example, and we're going to do a lot of detail, but a trend, for example, in the last few years, motivated by recession and the difficulty of selling, but then some people have discovered it's so profitable, they will stick with it long after there's a recovery, is it has driven a number of my clients back into the field. And one way or another, what were pure distance slash media marketers are not, they have a preview speaker out on the road, they're doing preview seminars, they themselves are going hither and thong and doing things, they're exhibiting at trade shows when they didn't exhibit at trade shows. So you really kind of like when you want to cut a government budget. The best thing here with process is you put everything on a table and reconsider everything. Now when you walk out of here, especially if you make modifications in drawer number one, you may want to make modifications in drawer number two. Last is the, oh, one other thing about that is we're going to talk about direct mail a lot today, that ought to be part of your process. Then the third drawer are the people we're selling to themselves, right? Are you investing in the best way possible to be talking to the best prospects possible? The greatest percentage of the time, who are we trying to sell to and then who are we trying to craft things for? So those are really the only three places in which we can do real work and we're going to talk about prospects first. So you will find a page, so realistic market assessment. If you read, and there's a couple pages that follow that page, if you read the Lyman Wood book, so how many of you got, what a way to make a living? Very good, okay. How many of you actually read it? Very good, okay. Cool guy, right? By the way, George, does that mean anything to you, Lyman Wood? Oh, we got to, hey, is Vicki in there? Oh, we got to get you this book. You would love this guy. So if you read that book, one of the key things about their success with the fellowship thing is they clearly understood there was a limit to how many they could affordably sell. They had a handle on the fact that there's only X number of people who are going to have a high enough level of interest in this thing. And if we try and press it wider, broader, deeper, all we're going to do is dilute the message that works with the core audience and we're going to lose money. So with niche marketers, this is even more significant because you're usually dealing with a more finite number to start with. And so I have this conversation with private clients all the time and often it's always an unpleasant conversation, you know, because they walk in the door thinking because there's 55,000 chiropractors that they have 55,000 potential buyers and it's nothing of the sort. I mean, that's just fantasy. First of all, some of them are too old. They are grandpas in practice in Podonk, Ohio. He's coming in one day a week. He hasn't gotten a new patient in five years, doesn't want one. He's just treating the ones he's already have until they all die. And then he's closing the doors and going fishing. He's not buying nothing. Some of them are too young. They're in the category we talked about yesterday to do we hide to know anything. They got out of school. Mama has a lot of money, so she set him up in a state of the art practice right away. They didn't go work for anybody and they don't know nothing. They think patients are going to materialize because they have healing hands and a bright smile. And it's going to be a while before we're going to sell them anything. And by the way, I haven't just described two isolated two people out of the 55,000. I've described a significant number out of the 55,000. There's other categories we can wipe out too, by the way. I can tell you, for example, that multi-doctor practices, more than three in a single practice in this particular niche, lots of luck making a sale to them. I can also tell you there's certain ethnic names you take off of a mailing list because lots of luck making a sale to them. And so you start to slice and dice this list. There's profound geographic biases that haven't changed in 35 years. There's six states you would never mail into if you were trying to sell to chiropractors because you aren't going to say, I swear to you, it's hopeless. So now when you bring this list down, you may have 25,000 out of the 55,000 that even ought to be considered suspects in sales language. Then we got to think about how many are anywhere near a mindset that will allow them to buy or allow us to even get them far enough along to buy. And there's a smaller number there. And so a realistic market assessment of, first of all, of what you got, what is the number? Because you got to build the economics of the business based on the real number. So if there really is only 25,000, give or take, you're going to get to 5% market share before your costs go through the rough. So 10% is 2,500. That gives us 1,250 people that we realistically, in that niche, can make a profitable sale to kind of the way we sell things. So now what does that business going to look like financially, right? And now we get to the second thing is the most important thing now is we got to figure out who the hell the 1250 are. Not by name, but by mindset. Where are the 1250? Because those are the ones we have to talk to. And we better figure out what the trigger point is with them. So now think about what most people do. They try and craft a message that will appeal to the 55,000. As broad, as fuzzy, political language is big tent, right? So in politics, right, you win in the primaries with the extreme core audience, which is why one of the reasons the process is so focaccta, but still it's instructive. So we're about to start, in case you haven't noticed, the presidential campaign starts on Monday for 2012. So far nobody's declared on the Republican side. Gingrich has declared he's raising some more money. That's all he's declared. God bless him. What a criminal he is. But so in politics, doesn't matter, Democrat or Republican, you win in the primaries because only the core, fervent, extreme of each party votes in primaries, you win by being a narrow, focused extremist. Now in the general election, of course, you got to cast this big, broad message and try to appeal to everybody, so you got to move back to the center in political language. Fortunately, in our kind of marketing, we never have to move back to the center, and generally speaking, we'll lose money if we do. So you got to think about, in my example, the 1250. Where are they? And again, if you look at Wood with their quasi-religion, right, A, they ain't trying to appeal to everybody. Their number's much bigger than the number I just described, but still, they didn't try to appeal, for example, to Catholics. Why? Because what they identified about their buyer, if you remember, actually I could probably get it exactly right, and we won't have to remember. And no, I don't think I have it. But basically, what they identified about their members were, their 1250, if you will, they wanted to pray, but didn't have any confidence in what to say. So they didn't know how to pray. Well, that rules out Catholics, because they get told what to pray. It rules out Lutherans. We got told what to pray. They hand you a prayer. So that rules out them, right? And so it's important to try and figure out, again, we'll use from my example, those 1250. What are they about? Because if you try and talk to the 25,000, you're going to miss the 1250. And the only place there's any real money for you is in the 1250. So like yesterday, we looked at these ads that I showed you, a 1910 example and a current example that are all about in the opportunity field, they're all about the adoring, admiring wife impressed with the husband who now is making a better paycheck or owns his own business or has his website online making money, right? And many of you, your visceral reaction to that, even though I showed you current examples, and as well as 1910 examples, your visceral reaction to that was it's as antiquated as grandma's ringer washing machine. That's not how men and women interact with each other and how the world works today. And it's probably not the way it works for any of you in the room who are married, particularly the women. That probably isn't you, right? If anything, it might be the opposite, right? You may be driving the ship and he's along for the ride, right? However, if you're selling money making opportunities to opportunity buyers and you want to know a little secret about the 1250, the overwhelming majority of them are still 1950 traditionalists and they're in conservative states. They are in Utah, Wyoming, Nebraska, Iowa, Ohio, Indiana, right? California is like a country unto itself. Purely by its density, you have to sell things to California and you have to change the message to work there. But otherwise, the opportunity adds, if you go get an opportunity magazine off the shelf today, which by the way, you should get them all and you should start paying attention to this, I noticed Walmart stopped carrying them all of a sudden for some reason, but if you go to Barnes & Noble, they're all there. From the rags all the way to the elegant stuff, you should get them all. Like at Pace Ad, you're going to see it in this month's magazine. Well, it's not because they're morons, it's not because the people running the company are stuck in the 1950s, it's because they're 1250, are still there. Mama's still in large numbers to stay at home mom, or if she's working, it's because they need to dough, not because they believe that that's the way things ought to work, and he's still about being the guy who brings home the bacon and gets the adoring dough-eyed look from Mama because he brought home a better paycheck. That's the 1250. And so if you modernize that message based on you, but you're aiming at them, you're in trouble, because that's still where they are. So your market assessment is a math assessment, how many there are, and it's a... It's a psychographic assessment of who they are once you decide how many they are. The other thing about this to understand, and many of you have heard this before, but I don't think I can say it enough, the overall numbers which in part define the affordably reachable segment of a marketplace, the overall numbers of American society, and I don't know, for those of you that came across ponds, but I would wager it's the same, I don't happen to have data, but in the United States, these numbers haven't changed, Social Security Administration started to chart them the year I was born, 1954. Everybody in the direct sales industry caught up and started to use them about 60, 61, 62. I first noticed them like 66, 67, 68. These numbers have not changed except in one place, which I'm going to show you. Otherwise they are the same, the last time Social Security Administration published them I think it's 2008. So they haven't changed all the way to 2008. They are the same damn numbers, and therefore they're pretty important. So here they are. The way it was always said, because 65 was the retirement age, and so the Social Security Administration still charts it this way. So if you follow them for their working career, age 20 to 65, so 45 years of work. Here's where everybody winds up. These are the old numbers, I'm going to show you the change. 1% rich, 4% financially secure, meaning they're not going to run out of money before they run out of life, which is an increasing problem of course in the stats, because damn it, they're keeping them alive longer. 54% are still working out a necessity, not by choice, and their total net assets, including their house, are under $100,000. 5% are dead broke, and 36% are dead. Now the only number that has changed between 1954 and 2008 is the 54 to 36, because they're living longer. So the 54% is now higher, and the 36% number is a little lower. Other than that, nothing's changed. I want you to think about everything that has changed that should have affected these numbers. Just think of all the gadgetry you've got in your little bags. We got cell phones. We don't have long distance. When's the last time you got to build a bunch of long distance charges on it, and anybody actually thought, well I don't know if we should call Aunt Martha this week or not because I don't have all those business calls outside of my area, no I can't make those, the long distance charges will kill me. When's the last time anybody had that thought? Access to information. George said, same thing, we're the same guy. New problem arises, new opportunity arises, go to the library. First of all, everything you want is there, and second, there's a little old woman sitting there who's dying to be helpful, who knows all kinds of shit, and will work for free. You go to the library, but you don't have to do that anymore. You don't have to leave your happy little couch, and your little pad, and you can access every piece of information known to man, as well as all the misinformation you would like, Wikipedia. Well, Wikipedia profiles, hilarious, mine included, very funny. I wish I was that interesting. But seriously, think of everything that has changed to remove barriers to achievement in that period of time, and we're in the same place. Even worse, arguably, because more people are living, and now more of them are in worse financial shape. The reduction of the death stat actually makes this a little worse. So all this change, and all of us, all of us, so in that period of time we had the whole self-improvement movement, S, life-spring, and everybody had spawned. We had the advent of public speakers who run around and tell people how to be successful. That business was literally birthed in the 60s. The First National Speaker's Association Convention was so small they had it in Cavett's living room, with space left over. So that's all happened. In the media, I always joke, it's harder to get stuff when the record player bounces on a car seat and the needle scratches, and it's hard to listen to them while you drive. So the cassette changed all that. And here we are with the same damn numbers. Now, here's what you've got to get based on that. You are not going to be the guy who changes the numbers either. Not you and not all of us collectively. They are going to be the same 50 years from now. Or worse, not better, and you aren't going to change them. And that pyramid is how people arrange themselves in every population in a very short period of time. So pretty much a parallel pyramid overlays every industry and every profession. I don't care. Fitness centers, mortgage broker while they're gone. Insurance salespeople, dentists, info marketers, speaker, the way I ever was. Same pyramid. 1%, 4%, etc. You go overlays. If you take a microcosm out of that industry and you organize them. So if we take all of, I see you've arrived, Michael. So if we take all of Michael Jans' PNC guys, who he has provided with marketing systems and sales tools and all that, so he's taken a microcosm out of the PNC insurance industry and organized them over here. Guess what? He's now in his customer base. Same damn pyramid. There's 1% of them kicking ass, taking names, using everything he's given them. Their incomes have multiplied. They just bought their second yacht. They got their second mistress. They got their third wife. They're just flying. There's 4% who are better. And then we start down the pyramid. If we go take the Forbes 500, which they publish every year of the richest Americans, go take a look at it. Their net worth is in it. It's 500 richest Americans. Take the net worth and build yourself a little pyramid. It's a pyramid. There's 1% of the 500 who are enormously rich. There's another 4% who are impressively rich. And then you're all the way down to the bottom of that 500. The guys who pretty much need to keep running their business still working just to cover their overhead. Now they got a big nut, but the same pyramid is there. So we are not ever going to impact, now we'll impact individuals because obviously we all probably, at least most of us probably started in one place of the pyramid and now we're in a different place. But by the way, most of us would have got there regardless of whether whoever influenced us existed or not because we would have found somebody else. The influences are really interchangeable. It's about the person being influenced, not really the influencer. So if I had enough money to go to college and become a veterinarian, instead of doing this, the impact on any of you or the world would be non-existent. It wouldn't matter. Because somebody else would have influenced you and you would have done what you did anyway. And the same thing is true of you, by the way. You are an interchangeable doohickey. If you went and did something else, ultimately all of Michael's P&C, ages 99% of them who are now in the 5% would have found their way to the 5% whether through him or whoever was his replacement. But we are not, so we move individuals up and down, but the numbers never change. Never. And so both process, profitability, business structure, it all, message, it all has to reflect the reality of this pyramid. If it is ever built under the delusion that it is somehow going to take the bottom of this pyramid and invert it, and that's how you're going to buy your mansion, that's delusional. Not going to happen. And by the way, the big, big, big, big movements who are all about that, namely Christianity, which is all about inverting the pyramid, that's the whole point, right? Losing ground, not gaining ground, especially here in the United States. So church attendance is down, donation, it's all down. It's all trending down. It's not trending up. So inverting the pyramid, insane. And you've got to be careful that nothing you're doing in your business is about the delusion of inverting the pyramid. Not going to happen. Our business has to be about finding the ones who are or can be developed into the top part of that pyramid. I'm going to skip over the Halbert stuff, but it all has to do with the power of the list. You can get to it on your own. So I'm all the way over to the stuff that looks like this about the Faith Healer's List, but I'll tell you one other thing before we get there. If you read the book, Sham, how many of you read the book, Sham? Oh, very good. Good for you. So in it, he was talking about his experience at Rodale. And very important. So I'm just going to read real quick. So, guided our entire approach at Rodale, the most likely customer for a book on any given topic with someone who had bought a similar book within the preceding 18 months. I have more, but so let's just stop there for a second. In list language, which in about another 45 minutes, where Craig's going to talk to you about direct mail list, in list language, that's recency. It's a select if you go get direct mail list. And so you can get the last 90 day buyer. You can get the last 12 months buyer. You can get the last 18 months buyer. With subscription lists, you can get the recent expires, people who expired and did not renew in the last 90 days, which often for many of us is the best list because they've given up on Guru A, but it doesn't mean they've given up on topic A. They just have given up on Guru A, which is why companies have portfolios of newsletters so they can move the expires to another one that they own. And that's what that business is all about, much like the faith healer list. So a person who had bought a similar book within the preceding 18 months. Now, first of all, it's important to know whatever comparable truth exists about your business and about your customer. So I just gave you one of the big truths that exist about all your businesses, which is the shape of the pyramid and the fact that it won't change. This may be a truth about your business, it may not, but there is a truth like it about your business and who the most likely customer is. And it's important to know the truth and then build everything for the most likely customer. So this gets us all the way to like length of message. So here's what everybody tells me. Nobody, just like George said last night, so he and I talk about this a lot because we refuse to be dissuaded. So nobody will watch, nobody will listen, they'll listen to those three tapes. This in business, I was doing with James, this is the test they had to take before they were allowed to buy. Plus, they had to listen to, and the husband and wife both had to sign in affidavit, that they had listened to 12 hours of audio material before they were allowed to buy. And if I were doing it today, I'd do exactly the same damn thing. I just did a very successful campaign, 46 page sales water, 50-some-odd page book, 90-minute audio CD, but oh no, we got it. Well, what do you do? You're shrinking everything for the willingness, the interest level of the unlikely buyers. That's what you're doing. That's like doing a spitch in a room for people who won't come in out of the parking lot. So what you'll do is go out and give the presentation, stand it on a car in the parking lot. Well, how effective is that going to be? No. So it's important to know who your most likely buyer is, and then you craft everything to influence the most likely buyer. You don't craft everything to influence the unlikely buyers. Screw them. Leave them in the bottom of the pyramid. Let somebody else rescue them. The money is in marketing to the most likely buyer. So like we're experimenting right now in a couple of niches. So Dean Kelley Beckworth, where are you? Okay. Gene is like, amongst other things, like the resident genius at birthday mailings, okay, including the fact of being able to get cold lists of people by their birthdays. So what do we know about birthdays? We know that people think differently. It's whoever said it first said, you know, I think it was Pat, and I don't remember, you know, that there's nothing like a near-death experience to focus your thinking. Right? Well, about 40 and up what are birthdays? They're increasingly near-death experiences. All right? And so they tend to focus one's thinking. All right? And so people behave in a little window around their birthday differently than they do at other times. And in certain industries, we've known it for a long time. Okay? So cosmetic surgery, cosmetic dentistry, okay, there's a significant number of patients who are at 40, 45, 50, 55, 60, 65 benchmark, okay, or people's behaviors changed by life event, right? So cosmetic dentistry, cosmetic surgery, for example, a significant number of female patients are in one of two categories, all right? They are dating or they married a younger, wealthier guy, or they're six months after a divorce and they're getting back in a game, and from the sales standpoint, we need them to have gotten the house. All that information is available. So I'm just beginning to run the experiment, and I can't believe I haven't done it before, in an opportunity environment, right? Because what's going to happen on benchmark birthdays? Charlie's going to think more that week than he thinks any other week that year about his status in life, about his income, his job, his unsatisfactory business, he's going to focus. And so I believe, I don't have empirical data for you yet, but I believe that if we take mailing lists that are successful and we take out of them only the people who are having their 50th and 55th and 60th birthday, and we shift all the resources to hitting only them, we're getting to more likely to buy prospects. Plus I can now tweak the message, maybe being obvious, maybe not being obvious, but I can tweak the message to press those buttons harder because they're exposed and I know what they are, which gives me a closed loop of improving effectiveness. Now it may be true that any birthday will bump response enough. It may not take a benchmark birthday, and maybe any birthday over the age of 40. 42 may work fine, 53 may work fine, I don't know. But I'm on the hunt, and I can't believe I wasn't on the hunt before. But this is who is this most likely to buy prospect? Because that's where we're getting all our money anyway. It's just you're wading through a whole lot of unlikely to buy prospects to find them and have them find you. Some part of what Solano said then, but the 18 month rule struck me as counterintuitive and discomforting in a self-help setting. Many of our books proposed to solve a problem. One would not expect people to need further help from us in the same problem area, and certainly not time and time again. I discovered that my cynicism was even built into the Rodale system in repurposing, reusing chunks of our material in product after product under different names. While discomforting or not, here's the big truth about that. People's dissatisfactions, people's problems are actually their hobbies. They are to them what golf or fishing is to you or horse racing is to me. That's why it doesn't matter how many books about it they buy, it's not ever going away. The person who has a hugely dysfunctional relationship based on one or more things, I'll bet you'll back me up on this. That's their hobby. They redo that again and again and again and again just like you go play golf every Friday. If you don't go for a few weeks you're uncomfortable. If therapy practice, if he stops them from doing it for three Fridays in a row, they're uncomfortable because you just took away their golf game. Whatever they're buying stuff in, they're going to keep buying stuff in. If you buy one prayer from a lot, how many damn prayers do you need? What do you keep buying prayers? It's like I always joked in my Peter Lo speech about the dragon to salesman to the sales seminar where they teach them 365 closes. Just give me one that works. I only need one. I'm only doing one sales presentation at a time, Bubba. I only need one that works. I don't need 365. But what do people want? One a week for 52 weeks times five years in a continuity program. Because not closed sales is their hobby. That's their thing. So the most likely to buy prospect has a dysfunction, has a hobby that he continues to buy stuff for. Because the poor stay poor. See, it don't matter how many opportunities they buy, the vast majority of them stay in the same place. That's unmoved, but not many. Most stay in the same place in the pyramid as they were. The faith healer list, which I put that in here for you, is a perfect example of both power of list and this whole thing about the same person buying over and over and over again and when they get tired of one guru, they just move to another. So in this example, all Roberts list goes to Pop-Off. Pop-Off's list goes to all Roberts. They're usually buying from more than one at the same time, by the way. So you can wrote it doesn't do any damage. We used to, I'll show you. So in US Gold, one of the ways, cost per sale went up and up and up, which they do over a period of time. So the back end was just them buying more stuff to sell, which of course, since most people don't do anything, the back end, not spectacular. But so we list exchange, because we're generating a lot of leads. And so we shared leads with another business opportunity marketer selling not the same thing, but a thing that had the same elements, a thing you took to a table somewhere and put it on a table and people walked up and saw the stuff and you made some form and they gave you money. So we swapped our leads real early in the sequences because we both wanted good leads, not dead leads. So our follow-up sequence was still going on when we gave our leads to Cassie and their follow-up sequence, although not as sophisticated as ours, was still going on when they gave us their leads. No damage. Everybody got what they were good at. Plus, as near as I could tell, 20% of the people were buying both and not doing anything with either one of them. Why? Because buying opportunities is their hobby. Not capitalizing on opportunity. That's a different hobby. Only 5% of the people ever have that one. So you can exchange. It doesn't do any damage. So you need to do a lot of thought about who's likely to buy and how can you focus and zero in on your most likely possible buyers and be wary of media devoting much resources, time, energy to media that does not allow you to do it. So, to a great extent, here's specific to this, here's my anti-Internet talk. To a great extent to me, particularly in this environment, much of what is done on the Internet is like for the local business billboard advertising. The hope is that, because 5,000 people drive by the billboard on the way to work every day, that there's a likely prospect in there somewhere and he'll see it. That's the hope. But I would rather know who the 52 likely prospects are in the 5,000 and go stick the billboard up in their driveway so they can't miss it. That's what I would rather do. And to me, it's exactly the same story. Now, there's also value issues, which I'm not going to get into. But so, if you want to recalibrate your thinking about this drawer prospects, you recalibrate your thinking to who is my most likely to buy prospect. What are they all about? What is their starting point? What is the one thing about them that I can hook to? So would the one thing was they want to pray and they don't know what to say, which by the way is poetic. So that one thing told them a lot about who not to bother with and who to bother with, how to craft the message. Because the message can be pretty clear, can it? If you want to pray and you don't know what to say, we're your guys. Now that rules out again, a whole lot of people. Immediately they want to say, my organized religion that I'm already a part of tells me what to say. So it repels them. I would wager. So I don't think a single Catholic in my family would ever answer his ad. I don't believe it. Certainly not the patriarchal mother figure of the family because she every time I'm there, she's telling everybody what to say when they pray. So they're not going to answer that ad. That ad's going to drive them off. Oh, well we don't want to drive off all those people. So we got to rewrite the ad so we don't, no, no, no, no. We want that guy, we want that person who right now wants to pray and doesn't know what to say. That's who we want. That's who we want it. So same game, same exact game. So your second drawer now, your second drawer is the thing and therefore what you can and can't say about the thing. So it's your actual product, your actual opportunity and the message which to me are fundamentally one and the same. If you want to separate them into sub drawers, feel free. So here are the types of things. We can have a thing that is replacement. So in a niche, usually that is a new, a new, seemingly new and different version of the same business or business within a business, an attachment to it. But we're not really taking them out of the niche. So we're not telling a chiropractor not to be a chiropractor anymore. We're just telling them what you thought was a chiropractic practice, that not what this is. This bag is a whole different kind of practice. That's new and radical and revolutionary and obsolete everything you ever thought you knew about chiropractic practice. So I wrote a headline the other day for a client. But if everything that is now part of your insert niche business is as obsolete as last year's phone. Well, it's an allergy because everybody's got one. You've seen that TV commercial now where all the people are POed as they're seeing the new thing right after they bought the thing. It's Best Buy's deal because Best Buy now is this malarkey program where they'll buy back your obsolete thing when you buy the new one. So you don't have to worry about buying the new one because it's called the obsolete in a few days. So they show somebody sitting there with their brand new doohickey and there's like a Steve Jobs character speaking to the group of them telling them that now this is version 8.2. But I just bought this yesterday. Yeah, well, so everybody's got this now, right? So I think it's good analogy, right? So replacement is in most cases, we're not actually taking them out of their business. We just want them to think we're taking them out of their business and everything they don't like about their business. Years ago I had a guy by the name of Rob Minton. He's in a real estate business. And here's how he starts his consulting day with me. I say, well, you know, tell me about your business, where you are, where you want to go? He comes up and got a piece of paper. He says, I got a list of everything I don't like about the business I'm in. I said, oh, great. So tell me the list, right? He's in a real estate business. He said, well, he said I hate doing listing presentations because, you know, at Best, four or five people, they wind up giving it to their sister in a lawn shed. I hate going to their houses at night. I don't like any part about doing listing presentations, all right? He said, and I don't really, I don't like doing open houses because you got to sit at open houses and you don't know if somebody's got a com or not and the candy and the thing. And they sit there all day on a Saturday and my wife bitch is about to leave. I don't like open houses, OK? I don't like being on call. I don't like people bugging me and the title company calling me that something didn't go through and I got to call them right back. I don't like any of that. I don't like showing houses because they're paying any ass. Oh, yeah, OK? Now, he's paying me, you know, at the time, I think my day fee at the time was like 12 G's so I got to try and keep a straight face for a while. You can laugh. And I won't bore you with the detail but basically when he's done, there's nothing left about the real estate business in his list except getting a check, all right? That's it. Everything else has been eliminated. He don't like any of it, all right? And I'm thinking, well, this could be a short day, you know? But so I say, well, I say, is there anything? And again, I'm trying not to say it in a tone of voice that is, you know, excessively sarcastic. I said, is there anything like you like, you know, other than cash in the checks? Anything at all, you know? And he says, well, I got a few investor clients and I kind of like them because they don't care if the countertops are green or blue or they only care about the numbers and they buy multiple properties during the year. So I don't have to go through all the work to get somebody and then I can't do any business with them again for five, six, seven years. He's selling multiple properties and I make double money because I make money when I sell to them and I make money when I sell it for them. So really everything is twice and pretty much they're pros. They're like business people so they don't bug me a lot, you know? So I kind of like them. It's fine, okay? So that's the business. That has to be the business. You're only going to deal with investors and you're going to not do anything else, right? Which is exactly now what we did. So everybody's walking around a list of all the stuff they don't like in whatever business they're in. They may not have put it down on paper but believe me, they got it in their head, okay? We all do. You do, okay? I do, okay? The list gets longer, by the way, with each passing year, you know? As soon as you solve a few and get them off the list, another 20, you know, have appeared. My list is different than it was 10 years ago but I got the list, right? And so everybody's got the list. And so really the structure of the replacement thing should be designed to wipe out the list. So whoever's in your niche, think about it this way. What's their list? What's their top 10 list that all you're likely to buy prospects would agree on they don't like about the business they're in? Now, obviously some individuals have different things on their list but there's some that are going to be on virtually everybody's list. Now let's make the replacement about not being on the list. So a big replacement that we did in chiropractic in the late 70s to give you an example. So replacement opportunity. At the time, 99% of chiropractors were 90% insurance compensated, all right? The entire practice was people were using their insurance. And unless that's from a train wreck, which not a lot of offices are located right by the tracks, but I did know one chiropractor is right by the tracks. And like four times a year there was a big train wreck or somebody's car got hit by a train, he couldn't have been happier. You know, it's like, oh great. 22 damaged people are all coming in the door. I mean they're literally crawling off the train crawling in the office, sign them up, you know? But you know, most people are not in that position, right? So everybody's tragedy is somebody's opportunity. So all these practices were insurance driven, right? Which the lazy doctors were all dependent on, but there's their list, okay? If you ask them for their list, they all had the same list, okay? We hate the insurance companies. We hate the paperwork. We hate them rejecting cases. We hate them coming back and saying, you want to bill us $56, we're only going to give you $18, they hate the caps, yada, yada, yada. This is a whole list, okay? That's it. This is a whole list, okay? So what's the replacement? Cash. Exactly, okay? The cash practice, which is what we called it. And so our replacement was no insurance. We're going to show you how to run a successful practice and make all the money you want and you accept no insurance, none zero, all right? If somebody wants to go get reimbursed on their own and fight with their insurance company, that's fine, but everybody's going to give you cash. Furthermore, since we're going to get cash from them anyway, let's get it in one big lump sum, so let's sell prepay. And so we taught prepay. So now if you've been to a chiropractor, you know the drill, right? You need to come in five times a week for the first six more of a few. That's all that gets calculated up, and no, it's a $13,800 case, and you can take care of it in one payment or three payment, and you save 20% because now you can give them discounts, which you can't do if you part cash, part insurance because you can't legally do anything for the cash patient. You don't do for the insurance patient, but if you're not taking any insurance, you can do anything you want. So yeah, you can save 20%. If you do one pay, you save 10% if you do three, right? So that was our replacement. Now I'll give you another example, same profession and dentist. So Greg Stanley, who some of you may know, very successful, practiced consultant for many years. He hit a wall in his business because some people have this problem, great brand name, great reputation, but everybody in the niche thought they knew what he was about. So if you stopped everybody at a trade show, chiropractor or dentist, and you said, what do you know about Greg Stanley? Without exception, they would start by saying good things. Good guy, good reputation, does what he says he's going to do, et cetera. And then they would tell you his program on a matchbook cover. He tells us to get our overhead down below 50%, pay off all our credit cards, live debt-free, invest in municipal bonds, and put the staff on his own program. That's his whole deal. Well, so who's coming to a seminar? Who's buying a program? There's no mystery why he hit the wall. I said, you got to keep the reputation and scrap the content, right? We need a replacement. So chiropractors and dentists, now instead of insurance, if you ask them to make another list about all their pains, the thing that aggravates them the most, that they're least happy with, what do you think's on the list? Number one on the list. Staff, absolutely. Number one, now they're going to tell you, because none of it's their fault, of course. Nothing going on in the office is their responsibility. So it's entirely the staff, right? And one of the big gripes about the staff is they never refer. And by the way, if you start to talk to doctors about referrals and why their referral rate is so shitty, which it is, they will immediately tell you, well, I got 18 staff people in here and they don't even bring anybody in from their family. And they don't encourage the patients to refer. And so we run a referral promotion and we put the little button on them, like the Herbalife button, lose weight, ask me how. We put the button on them and nothing happens. So okay, what's the replacement? We call it staff-driven practice. Here's the old pitch. Stop, forget advertising, forget marketing, don't spend any more money, which they don't want to spend that anyway, right? So I love that deal, all right? Stop it all. Don't be in Valpak. Don't do nothing, all right? Send your staff to us for three days and we'll return your staff to you as a referral machine. And they will drag people in off the street. They will bring their family in. They will talk to the patients for you. They will get the patients to refer and your practice will increase by 10% a month every month so it'll double every year purely based on the efforts of the staff. Replaces all practice. By the way, it's not an easy promise to keep. But as I told Greg, that ain't my job. My job's coming up with a replacement, right? Making the thing work is entirely your damn problem. I don't get paid enough to solve that too. But so those are replacement examples in which you really don't take the person out of the business, right? But you literally keep the name of the business and in his mind change the entire business, okay? The second thing that might be in this drawer would be true replacement. So like there's a very successful thing being run in chiropractic right now and the guy, literally the ad in the trade journal is all about getting out of practice, okay? The guy is teaching commodities trading to chiropractors. The whole pitch is a week's pay in four hours and never see a patient again as long as you live unless you want to, okay? Now I'm actually surprised the trade journal took it. Usually you have to do those kind of things by direct mail because the trade journals won't run the ads. But right now trade journals, magazines are taking ads they wouldn't take two years ago because they're a little short on advertisers. So he actually is doing a pure greener pastures pitch. And if he hit his stuff, the whole opening is everything that's horrible about being in practice and everything that's going to be horrible about being in practice and why I got out and I started doing this and 12 months later I was able to burn my practices to the ground and walk away. We actually used a similar pitch for Get Rich in Real Estate for Docs with Ben Altadonna program a few years ago. We didn't quite go that far but our star was a guy who was up to eight offices and they were driving him crazy and he was one step away from his order blowing up from the stress and he started to do real estate and then three years later he was able to sell his practices and now he just gets a check in the mail and sits on his boat so it's close to the same story. The reason this works is because everything looks better from a distance. Everything. And it really looks better from a distance in dim light, close to closing time. But everything looks better from a distance. In fact, if you have mixed dog mastermind groups, which most of you don't because you're in niches, but Bill has had them. So here's what happens in the mixed dog groups. At the end of the day, everybody wants to get in everybody else's business. And you actually had Mitt in selling to our group to get into his business. Yeah, sure. By the income for life thing, it's your second business. Yeah, because he was in the group too. So everybody wants to get in everybody's business. Even though they've actually described what they don't like. Because even their don't like list looks better than my don't like list. Right? I'm going, well that sounds shitty, but it doesn't sound as shitty as my shit. Right? And so what we call the greener pastures pitch, which is based on an Earl Nightingale story, which you undoubtedly all heard. So greener pastures, now obviously you may or may not want to do it, but it works. So you can go into your niche or another niche and you can move them to greener pastures much easier in many cases than you can sell them a better version of what they already got. So we had a guy in one of our groups. Who was in the group with Larry McIntyre? The dean you were in. Anybody else? Oh, you missed some fun. So you've seen this guy's stuff though. So Larry was in my VIP group I think for two years. And Larry's business was matching American men up with foreign brides. And so everybody in that business basically sells lists or now they sell access to a website and women put all their stuff. So it's like match.com but in the Balkans. But his business, well it's mostly Russian, it's a lot of Russian because they want to get out. This is shopping for green card on the other end of this deal. So they mostly went out of bad places. But so his business never was this. His business was big fee, kind of do everything for him. So you paid a fee and then they pre-screened the women and health checks and they all had to be Christians and profiles. And ultimately after you had corresponded with X number of them, Larry took a group to Moscow or to Japan or wherever this group was going and then they met with them. They were going to marry one. They got all the legal stuff facilitated for them. Larry was even a minister so we could marry him. So and in the time he was with us, I think he went from like under a grand, 900 bucks, I think we took his price up to 8,000 or 8,500 bucks by the way, with no difference in response. And we learned some things about who the most likely prospect was. So my conversation with Larry about that by the way started with, well who's your customer? Well, it's guys over the age of 40 and they're divorced. And look, here's my testimonials. There's ministers, there's truck drivers, there's doctors. There's, well, I see, but there's got to be more of one than of another, right? And he's doing big broad brush advertising, mostly in USA Today. Well, here's what we determine is the most likely prospect to buy. Three time divorce, long haul truck driver over the age of 50. This is the guy, right? He's a slam dunk, all right? He's buying, that's his number one, now that changes everything, right? It changes the media you advertise in, there's lists. At that time you could voice broadcast them and the trucking companies voice broadcasted them to recruit them. So there was a company who could voice broadcast them, there's truck stops where you could put literature, I mean, it changes everything, right? As soon as you know that fact, all right? And it really is a greener pastor, it's the ultimate greener pastor's say, right? So the guy is now giving up on, and to be clear about this, see, my theory is like at about the fifth or sixth divorce, the common ingredient in this problem, it ain't them, right? One, two, maybe, five, six, no, the common ingredient here. See, like Kelsey Grammer's right on the cusp here, with number four, the odds are excellent, it's him, okay? He's experimented with different ages, sizes, shapes, backgrounds, say so probably, you know. But so the greener pastor here is, this guy has concluded in his mind, right? So here's where he is, that not only is the problem the woman, it ain't the fact that he's on the road five days a week, only home on weekends, sleeping with hookers at the truck stops while he's on the road, drinking like a fish when he's, no, it's not that, it's the woman, and furthermore, it's because she's a damn American. Well, that's what he's concluded, okay? His feminism has screwed the whole thing up, okay? They're unmanageable, right? It's because they're an American, that's what he's concluded, that's where he's at, right? And now the greener pastor is Russian bride, Asian bride, so what does he have in a picture like about an Asian bride? That's exactly right, the picture he has in his head is complete obedience, he's thinking old James Bond movies, you know, she's in the kimono, the slippers, the long black hair, and she's giving him a massage in the bathtub and cooking him a meal, and she don't even speak English, perfect, right? That's, you know, that's what's in his head, okay? That's what's in his head, okay? Here's of course the problem, the flaw in that ointment should anybody be tempted is they aren't here very long, even if that's true when they get here, unless you are going to keep them locked in the basement, which there are laws about that, people try it, incidentally, but the problem is you brought them to America, that only would work on a deserted island, right? Because like we have TV, we have neighbors who say, what? You're doing what? What century are you from, girl? So in a very short period of time, here's what he's got, the fourth American wife with different eyes and a green card, that's what he's got, so the thing doesn't work for the most part, but it's the ultimate greener pastor's sale to the right person. Now think of the improvement alternative to that. So what's the improvement alternative we sell to that guy? Him, yeah, we sell him therapy. Well, please. Which one of these stories is a better story? You remember the little one you saw in the James Bond movies, giving him a rub down in the hot tub and cooking him a meal, who don't speak English? Or we could fix you. Which one of these things seems most appealing to you, right? And then if we match that with the soap, there are some people, I imagine, the improvement story would be more appealing because they would on some same rational level reject the opportunity outright. However, if we match the story up with the right guy, we got magic because he wouldn't take the improvement story if you were paying him to come to therapy. Not going to happen, right? I did that, by the way, and I didn't like it either. The only good news was the therapist concluded it was her. And mostly what he wanted to talk about was how to be a speaker and an author and publish a newsletter. Carla, by the way, was not thrilled. She picked the guy, I'm going, hey, he's your guy. You picked him. I'm happy to come. So it lasted like four visits and that was the end of that. But so think now about the replacement opportunity. So not only didn't we do improvement, we did a true replacement of greener pasture. So those are the two kinds of replacement, right? Then we have a kind of a step down is to talk in reinvention language rather than replacement language. So replacements harder to assemble and present. Although I've given you two examples of doing it in the chiropractic niche. And I could give you more, by the way. And I'm doing one in the financial advisor niche right now. We're talking replacement language. We're not talking reinvention language. And again, they don't like list. So anybody in here sell to financial advisors? Okay. Okay, well, both do. What do you think is real high on their don't like list? You guys want to know? Compliance. Yeah, they don't like compliance. Yeah, but okay, I'll narrow it further for you to help you because this is now useful to the two of you too. Well, that yeah, compliance home office. Yeah, they hate the home office, which by the way, they should. But so the ones who do seminars, who put people in seminar rooms and move them from seminars to appointments, they have a term they use. Play liquor, you got it. That's exactly right. Okay, they hate plate lekkers. Plate lekkers are people come to the seminars for the free meal. Okay, okay. And then, you know, pretty much were unqualified or had no intention of what kind of part but they go to all these seminars, by the way. So see seniors, which is who they're selling to. The first phenomenon you might want to know about seniors that you don't know. The next time you're in a casino, watch for this. Okay, there are seniors who haunt the casinos with a rewards card. And they asked to put it in your slot machine if you don't have a rewards card, because they are eating off the points. Okay, this is their supplement to social security. This is their retirement plan. Okay, they're in the casino all day stacking up reward points on other people's play. So they're mostly over at the dollar machines, by the way. They're not at the penny machines. And they're eating off the points. Okay, and Trump casinos in Atlantic City are the most generous. It's like 10 points to a dollar or something, that you can go into buffet and use them. So really, she's only got to work for a few hours and she can get a good meal. Okay, so seniors. So there are a bunch of seniors who go to all these seminars just to eat. Okay, I mean, there's at least a couple of them a week. So it's the night out. Right. I mean, they go and they have a nice meal. And some of them are feeding them steak for God's sake. So they all hate, they use the term and they hate them. Plate lickers, I hate plate lickers. Now it never occurs to most of them. There's a simple solution to this. Don't feed them. Simple. Now you'll have less people in the room, but so what? You should hate them. Why do you want them in the room? You don't want a person in the room who came for the meal. Right. There's other solutions, by the way, too, because one of the reasons I hate it is the money. So you can get somebody to feed them for nothing. But really, the thing is just don't feed them. Right. So I got a whole replacement business for financial advisors. It is all about that's it. I just gave you the whole thing. Right. The business is no more plate lickers ever seminars, no plate lickers. Guess what the four by six card is it has to be turned into 12 audio CDs and three days of training. Don't feed them. That's it. I just gave it to you. Right. And they're loving it. Right. That's full replacement. Okay. So you step down though is reinvention. So reinvention structure is essentially not as radical. Right. So like to go back to our cash practice example, that's too radical. That's true replacement. Okay. We're never going to take insurance again. We're not going to take insurance from anybody. We're not going to take a Medicare patient. We're not going to take a Medicaid patient. And we're only going to do cash. And we're going to do prepay. That's true replacement. So reinvention would be more about maybe how to only get the patients who have really good insurance. That might be reinvention. Reinvention might be how to get the patients to do most of the work to get the insurance reimbursed. Reinvention might be focusing only on a particular type of patient for who there are hardly any caps. Reimbursement is more generous than reimbursement for other kinds of patients. Right. Reinvention might be how to put an MD in your practice and have a DCMD practice so you can bill under MD codes instead of under DC codes, which are more generous and less likely to be turned down. Okay. So that's a step down from full replacement. It's easier to build your box. It's not as powerful. It's drifting from opportunity talk to improvement talk. Hard not to have the drift. Right. But it still can be pretty powerful because if we can take their don't like list and we can do some reinvention that maybe doesn't eliminate the list, but it knocks some stuff off the list and it makes some of the stuff not as bad. All right. Then we've done something important. Rearrangement, now we're taking another step down. We're sort of drifting even closer to improvement in a way from opportunity. Rearrangement now is really you're one step away from, you know, the deck chairs on the Titanic. Right. So rearrangement now gets us into changing the sales process, changing the way their new customer comes in the door, changing their advertising and marketing to produce a better customer, changing their pricing and their fee structure. So they make more money on each one. So they need less. Now that's the kind of puzzle pieces we're playing with on the tabletop. And when we get done, we have to present it as close to opportunity language as we possibly can. Then we have addition expansion. That gets us somewhat back to opportunity because we get to talk about business in business or business attached to business. So Donna's in the business in a business, business. So she's installing weight loss businesses in fitness gyms and other kinds of practices. So she's totally in this category. She is, you keep your list of shit, you know, like you leave your business the way it is. But man, we hook this thing to it and look how great this is. Right. So that's that gets us right back to opportunity. And we get to do opportunity speak. And we really are selling an opportunity. Right. It has synergistic relevance. So it's not like telling the guy with a fitness gym to open a car wash. Right. That's greener pastures. In this case, it's synergistic. Okay, it has relationship. But so in Kairos, for example, some years ago, we sold a whole package about how to put what really is today standalone franchise like massage envy, how to put that kind of a massage business in the office next door to your chiropractic practice. Synergistic chiropractic patient needs massage massage therapists can create chiropractic practices. Our wrinkle, by the way, is the only way you kept your place as a massage therapist, because you had patients given to you, you had your credit cards processed, you had your phones answered, you had your space. The only way you kept all that was you had to produce five new chiro patients a month for the chiropractic practice. It doesn't matter where you got them from, but you had to deliver five. Otherwise, your ass was out and the next one was in. So the pitch the doctor was, if you got five therapists, you're going to have 25 new patients a month. That's all you need. So not only is this a great business, it eliminates all your marketing of your other practices. And that cool. It was very cool. So the addition of business in a business or business attached to a business is another way to approach this. They are and it is leveraging assets, right? So somebody's already got customers. They already got maybe a physical location. They already got a staff. Maybe they already got salespeople. And even if they're all crappy, even if the whole thing sucks, they still got customers. So we can leverage what they already got by attaching this thing to it. And we get to tell an opportunity story to them instead of, right? So like the way to solve the United States Postal System problem is privatize the whole damn thing. And in every area, let retailers bid on who gets to have the post office in their store. But the government keeps all the money all the retailer gets is traffic. So if Walmart wants it fine, if CVS wants it, Costco wants it, we don't have a shit. Home Depot, I don't care. The brothel, they can have it. Whoever bids the most gets it. And what they get forward, that solves the whole problem. Never happened. But that's because in small towns, it is that way. In real small towns, the post office is in the back of the hardware store that still exists. So why does the hardware store owner like that deal? He makes a little bit of money on the post age, but it brings them traffic. Yeah. And it takes only a corner, which he was using. He only had back there was crap anyway. So that's the story, right? That's it. Okay. The last one is a multiplier. Again, this allows us to talk opportunity, right? It's a multiplier. So maybe the guys got eight jewelry stores. So maybe everybody that's got jewelry stores, we're selling them a system for party plans selling. And now he's going to put Tupperware like people out on the street holding home parties in a big geographic area around where his stores are. That's a multiplier effect. And maybe it'll multiply even more. Those of you who sell internet marketing. See, if you treat it this way to existing business owners, so you got something that works in retail here, now we can multiply it. We can be global, not local with affiliates. Now we can multiply it even more. And so now your one crappy little store is actually 50,000 stores with this opportunity, tapping into 50,000 people's customer basis. That's the multiplier story, right? So all the way back to go by the inch. So the sales letter, we didn't tell it in the show, but the package that you got had a multiplier story yet. So yeah, you can make that little money yourself or if you wanted to, you could have 10 locations in your town. And you can hire retired people and high school kids to sit at the location at the swap meet every weekend. And you can just run around and collect the money. So there's a multiplier story. So instead of making $500 from one, you can make 250 from 10 in the same weekend. Oh, that's pretty cool, right? And we even showed them three tier. So really now you could have 50 from which you only made 150. And you could have them suckers everywhere. So that's the multiplier story.
